summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a fundamental lesson learned while building Stack Overflow: users simply will not read anything on the screen that isn't absolutely essential to their immediate task. Despite providing toolbars, help buttons, formatting guides, and real-time previews on the ask question page, users consistently submit poorly formatted questions. This 'user myopia' affects everyone from casual power users to professional programmers. The solution, Atwood concludes, is to place critical guidance directly in the user's unavoidable line of sight—such as pre-populating form fields with example formatting—rather than relying on peripheral help elements that users will ignore.
key_points: Users will only read the absolute minimum text necessary to complete their task, ignoring all peripheral help and guidance
Even sophisticated users like programmers on Stack Overflow fail to follow formatting instructions despite multiple forms of assistance
The gap between how UI designers see a page (full of helpful tools) versus how users see it (just the input field) is enormous
Help elements must be placed directly in the user's unavoidable path, not in toolbars or sidebars they can ignore
Pre-populating input fields with example content may be more effective than any amount of surrounding documentation
money_quotes: The plain fact is users will not read anything you put on the screen.
Users do read. But users will only read the absolute minimum amount of text on the screen necessary to complete their task.
I can't quite explain it, but this kind of user myopia is epidemic. It's the same problem, everywhere I turn.
We're not particularly good, but we do try very, very hard not to suck.
Think long and hard about placing things directly in front of them, where they are not just visible, but unavoidable. Otherwise they might not be seen at all.
Those who can't learn from history are doomed to repeat it, I guess.
key_insight: Effective UI design must account for user myopia by placing essential guidance directly in the user's unavoidable line of sight, not in peripheral help elements they will instinctively ignore.
